metadata.title: Teaching with AI: A Practical Guide — Book Review
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown

tags: Teaching with AI: A Practical Guide — Bo
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The book grounds AI teaching in Deci and Ryan's needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness. It adds Bandura's self-efficacy sources: mastery, verbal persuasion, and social modeling. Sixteen pages explaining GPT, transformers, and neural networks may tempt many readers to skip. That theoretical grounding, not the tech primer, is the real upgrade for practice.

artifactLines: The book grounds AI teaching in Deci and Ryan's needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness.
It adds Bandura's self-efficacy sources: mastery, verbal persuasion, and social modeling.
Sixteen pages explaining GPT, transformers, and neural networks may tempt many readers to skip.
That theoretical grounding, not the tech primer, is the real upgrade for practice.

beats.narration_text: The framing on Deci and Ryan's autonomy, competence, relatedness and Bandura's self-efficacy is what I want to actually use in a lesson, not skip past. Help me turn theory into a class activity. Give me: (1) how the three self-determination needs would each show up in one AI-assisted assignment, (2) which of Bandura's four self-efficacy sources an AI tutor can realistically deliver, (3) whether the 16 pages explaining transformers matter for a teacher's practice.

beats.narration_text: Written by Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. Humanitarians AI is an educational bridge program where Fellows learn experiential AI by doing AI — building real projects and shipping real research.
